content: Listen, you know the drill: you spin up a TCP listener on port 443, hand it off to Tokio's TcpListener or std::net::TcpListener, and expect it to just pass traffic through. You build your proxy chain using tokio::net::TcpStream and wrap everything in Arc<Mutex<T>> so thread safety isn't an issue. But here is the trap that catches ninety percent of engineers when they try to implement SNI routing without understanding the actual protocol mechanics: you are assuming the hostname comes after the TLS handshake, when in reality, Server Name Indication arrives right at the start of the TLS record layer, before any encryption happens. If your code tries to read the host from a decrypted stream while the connection is still encrypted, you will get a panic or worse, you will just silently drop packets because the OS stack has already moved on. The SNI extension is actually optional in RFC 5606, but if it's present, it lives in the ClientHello message as part of the TLS record, which means your application layer must intercept the TCP stream exactly where the encrypted blob begins and parse the plaintext handshake fields using a crate like rustls or tokio-rustls. You cannot assume that once you have accepted the connection with accept_one(), the socket is magically decrypted for you; Rust's std::net::TcpStream gives you raw bytes, nothing more. So when a client connects, you receive a TLS record containing the ClientHello, which includes the SNI extension if configured. Your job is to extract that SNI field from the raw bytes before calling any decryption function like rustls::ClientHello::from_bytes or tokio_rustls::client::Config::connect. Most people try to do this by buffering data in a Vec<u8> and then dropping it into a connection pool, but if you don't inspect the ClientHello extension immediately upon receipt, your backend routing logic based on hostname will never trigger because the stream remains encrypted until the full handshake completes. The real gotcha is timing: you must parse SNI before invoking the decryption logic that would otherwise consume the entire TLS record. Imagine a scenario where you have multiple backends for different services; if your proxy code waits for the first few bytes to decrypt, it will only ever see the TLS record header and never know which service to forward to because the hostname is buried inside the encrypted ClientHello extension until the handshake fully resolves. The solution is to implement a small state machine in your Rust application that reads the initial TCP stream, checks if SNI is present by looking at the TLS RecordType values using rustls's internal APIs, extracts the hostname string from the parsed ClientHello, and then immediately spawns a new async task or thread to connect to the correct backend service. You will notice that even with crates like tokio::net::TcpStream or std::sync::Mutex protecting shared state, you still cannot bypass the TLS protocol rules; Rust does not magically unwrap encrypted data for you unless you explicitly parse the TLS record layers yourself. The key insight is that SNI routing requires your proxy to act as a partial decryptor at the very first byte boundary of the connection, inspecting the ClientHello extension before committing to any downstream network operations, and only then can you safely use the hostname to route traffic using std::net::TcpStream or tokio::net::UdpSocket depending on whether you need TCP or UDP passthrough. If your architecture relies on a single shared socket for all backends without this early SNI parsing stage, you will inevitably fail to distinguish between services, leading to either random backend selection or connection drops when the TLS handshake fails due to unexpected certificate expectations. So remember, in Rust specifically with its ownership model and async runtime, you cannot cheat the protocol; if the hostname is encrypted until the ClientHello, your code must explicitly handle that decryption step before attempting any routing logic using crates like tokio-rustls or rustls itself.
